one_line_takeaway: A first, careful red-team measurement showing GPT-4o is more robust than GPT-4V under text attacks but less safe unprompted, more exposed via the new audio channel to persuasion-style jailbreaks, and comparatively more vulnerable (though attacks remain mostly ineffective) to multimodal image jailbreaks.
key_finding: GPT-4o's safety is uneven and modality-dependent. Under text attacks GPT-4o is more robust than GPT-4V (e.g. AdvBench J4: GCG 19.2% vs 1.9%, AutoDAN 23.3% vs 3.5%), but with NO attack GPT-4o is less safe (RedTeam-2K Physical Harm J4: GPT-4o 24.3% vs GPT-4V 9.7%, a 14.6% gap in raw ASR). Persuasion-based attacks remain highly effective on both (BAP AdvBench J4 reaches 83.1% on GPT-4V, 73.3% on GPT-4o) and transfer to audio nearly intact (BAP Illegal-Activity: 100% text vs 80% audio), whereas suffix attacks (GCG/AutoDAN) collapse to 0% in audio because non-alphabetic adversarial symbols are lost in TTS. Known jailbreak templates give 0% ASR. Multimodal image attacks (FigStep, Liu et al.) are largely ineffective, often scoring LOWER than the no-attack baseline, yet GPT-4o is consistently more vulnerable to them than GPT-4V.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic empirical safety evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-4o against jailbreak attacks across three modalities (text, image, audio), using GPT-4V (GPT-4-vision-preview) as a comparison target. Its own contribution is not a new attack method but a measurement study: it applies 7 existing state-of-the-art jailbreak methods to GPT-4o/GPT-4V over 4 benchmarks and reports ASR under four different judgment functions, yielding four headline observations: (1) under attack, GPT-4o is more robust than GPT-4V in the text modality, yet without any attack GPT-4o is actually LESS safe than GPT-4V; (2) the newly added audio modality opens a new attack surface, and naively converting persuasion-based text jailbreaks (PAP, BAP) to speech still jailbreaks GPT-4o at ASR close to text; (3) black-box multimodal (typographic/semantic-image) attacks such as FigStep and Liu et al. are largely ineffective against both GPT-4o and GPT-4V, though GPT-4o is comparatively more vulnerable to them than GPT-4V; (4) well-known jailbreak templates (DAN, AIM, etc.) are fully neutralized (0% ASR), indicating OpenAI actively pattern-matches and blocks known templates.
threat_model: Red-teaming / adversarial attacker attempting to bypass the safety alignment guardrails of a commercial closed-source multimodal LLM (GPT-4o, GPT-4V) to elicit prohibited harmful content. Attacker access is black-box: only API (text+image in, text out) and the mobile app (for audio) are available; no gradients or weights of the target. To craft transferable text adversarial suffixes the authors attack a white-box surrogate model (Llama2-7b-chat) with GCG/AutoDAN and transfer the resulting suffixes to the black-box targets. Attack vectors span text prompts, typographic/semantic images, and text-to-speech audio.
limitations: Self-stated: (1) the audio attack merely translates known text adversarial prompts to speech, not exploiting GPT-4o's native audio understanding (intonation/emotion), so it understates audio-specific risk; (2) only text+vision multimodal combinations are studied due to scarcity of multimodal jailbreak datasets, leaving text+audio+vision combinations unexplored. Reviewer-observed: results are against a moving target (commercial models with dynamic, undisclosed defenses at a fixed 2024 snapshot), so 'ineffective' findings are time-bound; audio evaluation is tiny (200 samples, manually judged, no rate-limited API); no new attack is proposed and 'harmfulness' judgments differ wildly across J1-J4 (the paper itself notes J1 over-counts, J2 under-counts), so absolute ASRs are judge-dependent; some MM-SafetyBench scenarios (Financial Advice, Legal Opinion) inflate to ~100% ASR because their queries are weakly harmful and use lenient success criteria, not because of genuine jailbreaks.

datasets_used: RedTeam-2K (text; incorporates BeaverTails, Bai et al., Shen et al.)
AdvBench (520 harmful text queries; text modality + audio subset)
SafeBench (multimodal, built with FigStep typographic method)
MM-SafetyBench (multimodal, built with Liu et al. query-relevant image method)
quantitative_results: Scale: 2000 unimodal text queries / 15 scenarios; 2180 multimodal queries / 13 scenarios; >4000 optimized initial queries; ~8000+ analyzed responses
AdvBench text J4 No-Attack: GPT-4V 9.2%, GPT-4o 1.9%
AdvBench text J4 Template-based jailbreak (TBJ): 0.0% for both GPT-4V and GPT-4o
AdvBench text J4 GCG: GPT-4V 19.2%, GPT-4o 1.9%
AdvBench text J4 AutoDAN: GPT-4V 23.3%, GPT-4o 3.5%
AdvBench text J4 PAP: GPT-4V 69.2%, GPT-4o 62.7%
AdvBench text J4 BAP: GPT-4V 83.1%, GPT-4o 73.3% (highest text-modality ASR)
AdvBench text J1 BAP: GPT-4V 71.7%, GPT-4o 71.5%
RedTeam-2K J4 Physical Harm (no added attack payload): GPT-4V 9.7%, GPT-4o 24.3% (~14.6% gap)
RedTeam-2K J4 Government Decision: GPT-4V 24.3%, GPT-4o 34.9%

Audio (AdvBench subset, manual ASR): Ori-text/GCG/AutoDAN via audio = 0% on GPT-4o; BAP Illegal Activity 100% text vs 80% audio; PAP Illegal Activity 100% text vs 90% audio; Physical Harm BAP 100% both text and audio
SafeBench (FigStep) J4: GPT-4o consistently >= GPT-4V (e.g. Malware Generation 48% vs 6%); No-Attack ASR often exceeds under-attack ASR (attack largely ineffective)

MM-SafetyBench (Liu et al.) J4: weak-harm scenarios approach ~100% (Financial Advice GPT-4V 99.3% / GPT-4o 99.4%, Political Lobbying 88.5% / 98.1%); genuine-harm scenarios show attack ASR <= No-Attack ASR
baselines_compared: No-Attack (original query) baseline vs each jailbreak method
Template-based jailbreak (6 templates: DAN 7.0, DAN 8.6, AIM, Dev Mode v2, Text Continuation, AntiGPT)
BAP (bi-modal adversarial prompt, used for text optimization)
Four judgment functions as evaluators: J1 string-matching, J2 Llama Guard, J3 ChatGPT-judge, J4 GPT-4o-judge
